extra.message: User-controlled 'p' query parameter is passed directly to os.path.join() and open() without sanitization. An attacker can use '../' sequences to read arbitrary files outside the intended base_path directory (e.g. '../../settings.py').

User-controlled 'first_name' POST parameter is base64-decoded and passed directly to Python's exec() function. This allows arbitrary remote code execution on the server. An attacker can execute arbitrary Python code by providing a base64-encoded payload.

User-controlled 'qs' query parameter is rendered into a JavaScript string context using the |safe filter (bypassing Django auto-escaping). An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript by breaking out of the string with a quote character. Source in views.py:110 passes request.GET.get('qs') to the template.

User-controlled 'qs' query parameter is rendered directly into an HTML input value attribute using the |safe filter. An attacker can break out of the attribute with a double-quote character and inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript. Source in views.py:98 passes request.GET.get('qs') to the template.

User-controlled URL path parameter is rendered into a JavaScript string context using the |safe filter. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript by crafting the URL path to break out of the string. Source in views.py:104-105 passes the URL path parameter to the template.

User-controlled 'qs' query parameter is rendered into a JavaScript string context using the |safe filter. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript by crafting the qs parameter to break out of the string. Source in views.py:166 passes request.GET.get('qs') to the template. This is compounded by the @csrf_exempt decorator on the view.
User-controlled 'url' query parameter is passed directly to Django's redirect() function with no validation. An attacker can redirect users to arbitrary external URLs, enabling phishing attacks.

User-controlled 'fwd' query parameter is used to look up a function by name in globals() and call it. An attacker can call any view function, including 'admin', bypassing all access controls. This effectively provides an authentication bypass and function-level access control bypass.

The dor_user_profile function accepts a userid from the URL (e.g. /direct-object-references/users/2) without any authentication or authorization check. Any user can read and modify any other user's profile by simply changing the numeric userid in the URL. There is no session check or ownership verification.

The missing_access_control function grants admin access based solely on the 'action' query parameter value being 'admin'. No authentication or session check is performed. Any unauthenticated user can access the admin page by setting ?action=admin.

CSRF middleware (CsrfViewMiddleware) is commented out in MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES. This disables CSRF protection for the entire application, making all POST endpoints vulnerable to Cross-Site Request Forgery. Combined with @csrf_exempt on csrf_image view, there is no CSRF protection anywhere.
The csrf_image view is decorated with @csrf_exempt, which disables CSRF protection for this specific view. An attacker can craft a cross-site request that triggers actions via this endpoint.

DEBUG and TEMPLATE_DEBUG are set to True. In production, this will leak sensitive information including stack traces, local variables, Django settings, and database credentials when an exception occurs. Combined with the 'boom' view (views.py:143-144) which raises an unhandled exception, this exposes full application internals.

The Django SECRET_KEY is hardcoded in settings.py. This key is used for session signing, CSRF token generation, and other cryptographic operations. Exposure of this key allows attackers to forge session cookies and CSRF tokens, bypassing authentication.

XFrameOptionsMiddleware is commented out in MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES. This disables clickjacking protection for the entire application, allowing an attacker to embed the site in an iframe on a malicious page.

cwe: CWE-1021: Improper Restriction of Rendered UI Layers or Frames

extra.message: The 'monster' cookie is set without httponly, secure, or samesite flags. Setting a cookie without httponly=True allows JavaScript to access it via document.cookie, making it stealable via XSS. Without secure=True, the cookie is sent over unencrypted HTTP connections.

The Django Debug Toolbar can be enabled by passing 'debug' as a query parameter, POST parameter, or cookie value. An attacker can enable the debug toolbar to view sensitive information including SQL queries, template context data, HTTP headers, and Django settings.

The 'boom' view raises a raw Exception with no error handling. With DEBUG=True in settings.py, this will expose a full Django stack trace including local variables, source code snippets, and settings (including SECRET_KEY and database config).

cwe: CWE-209: Information Exposure Through an Error Message
